gate_claim: the adaptive-node guard-prologue Layer-1 integrity fast-path is correct and preserves per-mutation tamper refusal: the per-mutation --resume-check subprocess spawn is replaced by an in-process recompute of the plan bytes' plan_hash (via the plan-validator's exported computePlanHash) compared against the frozen embedded plan_hash marker, a match skips the subprocess and a mismatch OR any error falls back to the full --resume-check exactly as before, the recompute covers the SAME hash-covered bytes computePlanHash covers (a narrowed region would silently weaken tamper detection), Layer 2 (consent-halt fence) and Layer 3 (live-coordination) are byte-unchanged, the crash-repair path (reconcile-running-set) still runs the full resume-check, a post-freeze-tampered frozen plan STILL refuses plan_integrity_failed with ZERO mutation before every mutating opener (proven RED-first in test-adaptive-node.js), a matching-hash plan takes the fast path and does NOT spawn the subprocess (the dedup is real), and the four adaptive-node editions stay in exact parity (canonical + codex byte twin + gitlab/gitea rename-normalized ports)
gate_surface: the n3-adaptive-node-hashguard diff vs run base 0a9f652a — the four adaptive-node editions' mutationGuardPrologue L1 and the new tamper + fast-path unit coverage in scripts/test-adaptive-node.js, reviewed for hash-region correctness and fallback completeness and cross-edition parity

## What I re-executed (all from /Volumes/WorkspaceA/ylminiserver/workspace/kaola-workflow/.kw/worktrees/issue-725)

1. `node scripts/test-adaptive-node.js` — "adaptive-node tests passed (2487 assertions)", exit 0. (Stray Chinese-locale git stderr lines come from a pre-existing fixture spawning git in a temp dir; unrelated to this diff.)
2. `node scripts/validate-script-sync.js` — OK: 22 common scripts, 28 byte-identical groups, 5 rename-normalized families, 2 hooks.json families, 7 forge export-superset families in sync.
3. `node scripts/edition-sync.js --check` — "12 forge aggregator ports in parity with canonical." Additionally `cmp` proved the codex twin (plugins/kaola-workflow/scripts/kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js) byte-identical to canonical.
4. RED proof A (pre-change behavior), re-executed in an isolated scratchpad copy of scripts/ with the L1 hunk reverse-applied — repo untouched: #725a FAILS with exactly the shell-call list n3 recorded (the old L1 always shells --resume-check). A control run of an UNMODIFIED scratchpad copy showed the only other failure (D444-GUARDS) is a harness-environment artifact of running outside the repo root (it fails in the unmodified copy too), so the revert delta is exactly #725a — the RED is genuine.
5. RED proof B (naive marker-trust), scratchpad copy with the recompute replaced by hashMatch=true when a marker is present: #725b FAILS on all three of its assertions (tampered node wrongly opens, mutation occurs, no fallback subprocess) and the pre-existing #499b tamper test FAILS as well. The new coverage — and independently the old #499b coverage — genuinely binds the true-recompute property; a marker-trust fast path cannot pass the suite.

## Verification of each claim element

- Same hash-covered bytes: the fast path calls the validator's exported computePlanHash on the full plan content read from disk (scripts/kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js:8340), the exact function revalidateForResume uses (scripts/kaola-workflow-plan-validator.js:3574-3575). No re-implementation, no narrowed region — the primary hazard named by the gate direction is absent by construction. Read-encoding parity holds: both the adaptive-node CLI (fs.readFileSync utf8, adaptive-node:11223) and the validator CLI (plan-validator:4046) read utf8, so no false-match from read divergence is possible.
- Fallback completeness: hashMatch starts false, is set true only on a successful recompute-equals-stored comparison, and the whole read/extract/recompute is wrapped in try/catch forcing hashMatch=false on ANY error; a missing marker leaves stored null and hashMatch false. Every non-match path reaches the identical pre-change shell(validatorPath, [planPath, '--resume-check', '--json']) + plan_integrity_failed refusal, before any mutation (the prologue returns the refusal ahead of every mutating body). A caller passing no readFile throws into the catch and degrades to the always-shell pre-change behavior — fail-safe.
- L2/L3 byte-unchanged: the entire canonical file diff vs 0a9f652a is ONE hunk (+22/-3) inside mutationGuardPrologue Layer 1; Layers 2 and 3 and everything else are byte-identical. Same single hunk in all three ports.
- Crash-repair path unchanged: runReconcileRunningSet (adaptive-node:10384) has zero diff hunks — byte-identical to base. Precision note on the claim wording: reconcile's own body shells --barrier-check/--drop-base but has never itself shelled --resume-check (neither at base nor now; its repair guidance routes the operator back through orient, which shells the full --resume-check at adaptive-node:4896, unchanged). So "still runs the full resume-check" is loose phrasing in the claim/n3 evidence; the operative fact — the candidate does not weaken the crash-repair path in any way — holds exactly. Pre-existing behavior, not a candidate defect. runMirrorProject's own copied-plan --resume-check (adaptive-node:5324) is also unchanged.
- Tamper refusal before every mutating opener: prologue call sites with integrity:true are runOpenNext (:5380), runOpenReady (:8959), runCloseNode (:9872) — all mutating openers/closers retain the layer. Proven RED-first per items 4-5 above; #725b additionally asserts zero mutation (the tampered node stays pending) and that the mismatch reached the authoritative subprocess.
- Dedup is real: #725a asserts the open succeeds AND zero --resume-check shell calls occur on a matching-hash plan, with the stub deliberately returning ok for resume-check so a regression surfaces via the call-count assertion rather than a spurious refuse — the assertion genuinely binds.
- Cross-edition parity: the three port hunks are identical to canonical except the correctly forge-renamed require (kaola-gitlab-workflow-plan-validator / kaola-gitea-workflow-plan-validator); both sync validators green as above.
- Walkthrough #387: not re-run (n3 ran it green; ~19 min). Diff reading gives no reason to doubt it: the walkthrough's tampered-plan assertion exercises the mismatch path, which is byte-preserved fallback-to-subprocess against the REAL validator, and the suite-level equivalent (#499b/#725b) re-passed here against the real computePlanHash.
- Scope: the working tree vs 0a9f652a contains exactly the five n3 files plus the already-gated n1 pair (scripts/edition-sync.js, scripts/test-edition-sync.js — n2-approved, out of this gate's surface). Nothing else.

## Non-blocking observations (no admitted findings; recorded for visibility)

1. Marker-extractor case divergence, newly load-bearing: the fast path extracts the marker via adaptive-node's pre-existing planHashFromContent (adaptive-node:2992, case-insensitive regex + toLowerCase) while --resume-check uses the validator's case-sensitive readStoredHash (plan-validator:2582). A hand-edit that uppercases the embedded hex without changing its value would fast-path-match where the subprocess would refuse plan_not_frozen. Not admitted: the recompute over the same bytes matching the stored value proves the hash-covered content intact in that scenario, so no tampered content is admitted — only a marker-format pedantry refusal is lost, and the trigger (value-preserving case mangling of 64 hex chars) has no plausible accidental cause. injectHash always writes lowercase.
2. Accepted semantic delta inherent to the claimed design (not a defect vs the claim): on a genuine hash match the skip also bypasses revalidateForResume's residual non-hash checks — the filesystem-dependent installed-roles check and, when an epoch contract is active, the Required Agent Compliance form check (that section, like the ledger, is legally mutable and not hash-covered). All content-derived residual checks (contract, parse, MAX_NODES, cycle, sink) are functions of hash-covered bytes and passed at freeze, so a true match loses nothing there. Mid-run environmental drift (e.g. a role uninstalled after freeze) is now caught at orient (full resume-check, unchanged) and at dispatch rather than at every opener. The frozen gate claim explicitly specifies match-skips-subprocess semantics, so this is the approved design boundary, noted for the record.

n3's evidence file (kaola-workflow/issue-725/.cache/n3-adaptive-node-hashguard.md) was read first; its RED/GREEN narrative, command outcomes, and write-set accounting all reproduced or verified exactly, with the two wording precision notes above.

review_conclusion: The Layer-1 in-process plan-hash fast path is implemented exactly as claimed — same computePlanHash over the same bytes, fail-safe fallback on mismatch or any error, single-hunk change leaving L2, L3, and the crash-repair path byte-identical, tamper refusal proven genuinely RED-first by re-execution in isolated copies, the dedup pinned by a zero-subprocess assertion, and all four editions in verified parity — so the gate passes with zero blocking findings.
